caps.reason: You cannot be placed abroad without a passport, so your readiness is held at needs prep until you have one in hand. It is the first thing to sort, and SA passport turnaround is usually a few weeks.

reason.BEng / BSc(Eng) (4-year professional deg: A 4-year BEng falls under the Washington Accord, the strongest base for overseas recognition.
reason.BTech / BEngTech (3-year technologist): A BTech maps to the Sydney Accord technologist track, recognised but a notch below the BEng professional route.
reason.National Diploma (3-year technician): A National Diploma sits on the Dublin Accord technician track, with narrower overseas demand than a full degree.
reason.Honours / Postgrad in engineering: A postgraduate engineering qualification strengthens credential recognition and senior applications.
reason.Master / PhD in engineering: A Master or PhD is a strong differentiator for senior and research-led roles abroad.
reason.Currently studying: Finish your qualification first, as overseas assessors require a completed accredited degree.
reason.Non-engineering degree: A non-engineering degree will not pass an Engineers Australia or Ecctis competency assessment without a recognised engineering qualification.

reason.Pr Eng (Professional Engineer): Pr Eng is the top ECSA category and unlocks the SAICE to ICE chartered route in the UK.
reason.Pr Tech Eng (Professional Engineering Te: Pr Tech Eng is a strong professional registration recognised across Sydney Accord economies.
reason.Pr Techni Eng (Professional Engineering: Pr Techni Eng is a recognised technician registration, useful for technician-stream visas.
reason.Candidate (Candidate Eng / Tech / Techni: Candidate status shows you are in the ECSA pipeline but professional registration carries far more weight abroad.
reason.Graduate (not yet registered): You are not yet ECSA-registered, so start the candidate process to build toward professional status.
reason.Lapsed: Your ECSA registration has lapsed, so reinstate it before relying on it for overseas applications.
reason.Not registered: Without ECSA registration you lose the easiest evidence of competence for migration assessors.

reason.Outcome letter received: Your Engineers Australia outcome letter is the gold-standard Washington Accord assessment, valid for three years.
reason.Completed: Your Engineering New Zealand credential check is complete, clearing the NZ recognition step.
reason.Reference letter received: Your Ecctis UK ENIC reference letter is in hand, the credential step for the UK Skilled Worker route.
reason.ECA letter received: Your WES assessment letter is in hand, the credential step for Canada Express Entry.
reason.Submitted — in progress: Your overseas competency assessment is underway, which typically takes a few months to reach an outcome.
reason.Applied — in progress: Your overseas competency assessment is underway, which typically takes a few months to reach an outcome.
reason.Not started: No overseas competency assessment is underway yet, the key step that converts your SA qualification into a recognised credential.

rules.empty_reason: No destination competency assessment recorded. Starting the right assessor for your target country (Engineers Australia, Engineering NZ, Ecctis UK or WES Canada) is the key step that converts your SA qualification into a recognised credential.

reason.Civil: Civil engineering is a global shortage discipline, with strong demand across Australia, NZ, the UK and Canada.
reason.Structural: Structural engineers are in acute demand, particularly in Australia and the UK infrastructure pipeline.
reason.Mechanical: Mechanical engineering is broadly in demand across most destinations and industry sectors.
reason.Electrical: Electrical engineers are a priority shortage occupation in most Accord economies.
reason.Electronics / Telecommunications: Electronics and telecoms engineering is well demanded, especially in tech-heavy markets.
reason.Chemical: Chemical engineering is in solid demand, particularly in resource and process-heavy economies.
reason.Industrial: Industrial engineering demand is steadier but more competitive than the core shortage disciplines.
reason.Mining: Mining engineering is highly sought in Australia and Canada, where SA experience is a genuine advantage.
reason.Mechatronics: Mechatronics sits at the automation frontier and is a fast-growing shortage area.
reason.Aerospace / Aeronautical: Aerospace roles are concentrated in specific hubs, so target destinations with an aerospace sector.
reason.Geotechnical: Geotechnical engineering is in good demand on major infrastructure and mining projects abroad.
reason.Environmental: Environmental engineering demand is growing but more variable by destination than the core disciplines.
reason.Materials / Metallurgical: Materials and metallurgical roles are demanded in resource economies but narrower than mainstream disciplines.
reason.Other: Confirm how your discipline maps to the target country's skilled occupation list, as demand varies widely.

rules.empty_reason: No engineering discipline recorded. Shortage disciplines such as Civil, Structural, Mechanical, Electrical and Mining get hiring priority abroad, so add yours.

ranges.reason: Under 2 years post-graduation, most skilled visas and professional registrations need more experience.
2 to 5 years experience meets the bar for most skilled migration routes.
5 to 10 years experience makes you a strong applicant for competitive engineering roles.
10+ years experience positions you as a senior-level engineer abroad.

reason.Valid — 2+ years remaining: Passport valid with 2+ years remaining, which meets typical work-visa requirements.
reason.Valid — under 2 years remaining: Passport valid but renew soon, as most work visas need 2+ years remaining.
reason.Expired: Passport expired, so renew it before lodging any visa application.
reason.No passport: No passport yet, the first blocker to clear before any overseas engineering move.

reason.Current (within 6 months): Current SAPS clearance, valid for the 6-month window most visa lodgements require.
reason.Older than 6 months: Your clearance is older than 6 months, so most visa applications will require a fresh one.
reason.Applied / in progress: Your SAPS clearance is in progress, so time it to land near your visa lodgement.
reason.None: No police clearance yet, so apply now as SAPS turnaround can run several weeks.

reason.Under R50k: Below the typical R80k to R200k engineering relocation budget for assessment, visa and settling costs.
reason.R50k – R100k: Covers entry-level relocation costs but tight once assessment and visa fees are paid.
reason.R100k – R200k: A comfortable relocation budget for most single-applicant engineering moves.
reason.R200k – R400k: A strong budget that also helps with Canada's proof-of-settlement-funds requirement.
reason.R400k+: A very strong financial position for relocation, including family moves.
reason.Prefer not to say: Budget not declared, so share it privately with us if it shapes your destination options.

reason.Conversational: Conversational English will need a strong IELTS or PTE score to clear skilled-visa language bars.
reason.Fluent: Fluent English is typically sufficient for most engineering destinations once a test confirms it.
reason.Native / first language: English as a first language comfortably meets the language bar for all major destinations.

reason.IELTS Academic (general purpose): Standard IELTS Academic is widely accepted but not valid for the UK Skilled Worker visa, so check your target.
reason.IELTS for UKVI (UK Skilled Worker): IELTS for UKVI is the correct test for the UK Skilled Worker route.
reason.IELTS General Training (Canada Express E: IELTS General Training is the right test for Canada Express Entry.
reason.PTE Academic: PTE Academic is accepted by Australia and the UK but not by Canada Express Entry.
reason.None yet: No English test on record yet, which most skilled-visa routes will require.

ranges.reason: No dependants, the simplest profile to relocate with no dependant visas needed.
One or two dependants adds dependant visas and settling costs but stays manageable.
Three or more dependants means higher visa, schooling and settling costs, so budget and plan carefully.

reason.As soon as possible: An ASAP timeline is realistic only once your competency assessment is in hand, so start it immediately.
reason.Within 6 months: A 6-month horizon is tight given assessment lead times, so prioritise the assessor application now.
reason.Within 1 year: A 1-year horizon fits well with assessment, registration and visa processing.
reason.Within 2 years: A 2-year horizon gives ample room to complete assessments and strengthen your application.
reason.Just exploring: Exploring only, so use the time to plan the assessment and registration sequence strategically.
